metadata.summary: Multi-layer caching (browser → CDN → gateway → app → DB) with five patterns and Redis/Memcached production rules. Every cache layer needs explicit TTL, invalidation trigger, and stampede control.

content.title: Caching Architecture and Patterns
content.summary: Design multi-layer caching strategies (browser → CDN → API gateway → application → database) using the correct pattern per data type: cache-aside, read-through, write-through, write-behind, or write-around. Every cache layer requires explicit TTL with jitter, an invalidation trigger, and stampede control. Always measure hit ratio and p95 latency before and after.

why.p: Proper caching reduces p95 latency by eliminating redundant DB/upstream calls (70-90% fewer queries is typical) and absorbs traffic spikes without scaling the backend. Cache invalidation is the hard part: TTL is the safe default; event-driven invalidation only works with reliable change-data capture. Hot-key skew and stampedes are the most common failure modes at scale.

list.item: Read-heavy workloads with measured DB or upstream pressure (>30% of latency budget on data fetch).
Hot, repeatable read paths: profiles, catalogues, configuration, feature flags, auth lookups.
Cost reduction where compute/IO cost is dominated by repeated queries.
CDN/edge work for static assets, public APIs, marketing pages, image variants.
LLM-powered apps needing prompt cache, embedding cache, or tool-result cache layers.
Strong-consistency-critical paths (ledger balances, inventory at checkout) without write-through or distributed-lock pattern.
Personalized data with hit-rate ceiling below ~30% — the cache adds latency without payback.
Premature optimization: skip until profiling shows a hot key/query.
Writes-dominated workloads — caches become invalidation sinks.

section.name: cache-layers-overview
section.p: A complete caching strategy spans multiple layers, each with specific tools and TTL guidelines:

list.item: Browser: static assets, API responses. Tools: HTTP headers, Service Worker. TTL: 1h–1y.
CDN/Edge: static files, HTML, API responses. Tools: Cloudflare, CloudFront, Fastly. TTL: 5m–24h.
API Gateway: full responses, rate limiting. Tools: Kong, nginx, Envoy. TTL: 1m–1h.
Application: objects, queries, sessions. Tools: Redis, Memcached, in-memory. TTL: 1m–24h.
Database: query results, prepared statements. Tools: built-in query cache. TTL: auto-managed.

section.name: pattern-1-cache-aside
section.p: Use cache-aside as the default pattern for read-heavy workloads where the application controls cache lifecycle explicitly.

rule.statement: Use cache-aside as the default pattern for read-heavy workloads where the application controls cache lifecycle explicitly.
rule.rationale: Application code controls exactly what is cached and when; cache contains only requested data, avoiding prefill waste. Most flexible — compatible with any storage backend.

list.item: Read flow: App → Cache (miss) → DB → Cache (store) → App.
Write flow: App → DB → Cache (invalidate or TTL expiry handles it).
Best for: read-heavy workloads, data that can tolerate staleness, heterogeneous data access patterns.
Avoid for: write-heavy workloads where the cache is constantly invalidated, real-time consistency requirements.

section.name: pattern-2-read-through
section.p: Use read-through when you want transparent cache management — the cache library handles DB fetches on misses, not application code.

rule.statement: Use read-through when you want transparent cache management — the cache library handles DB fetches on misses, not application code.
rule.rationale: Removes cache-population logic from application code; all reads go through a single path. Simplifies code at the cost of flexibility.

list.item: Read flow: App → Cache (miss) → Cache fetches from DB → App.
Best for: simpler application code, consistent caching logic across all read paths.
Avoid for: complex invalidation requirements, data needing custom transformation before caching.

section.name: pattern-3-write-through
section.p: Use write-through for data that is frequently read immediately after write and where consistency is critical.

rule.statement: Use write-through for data that is frequently read immediately after write and where consistency is critical.
rule.rationale: Every write goes to cache and DB synchronously; cache is always warm and consistent. The latency penalty on writes is the trade-off.

list.item: Write flow: App → Cache → DB (synchronous).
Best for: data consistency critical (e.g., shopping cart, user preferences read immediately after update).
Avoid for: write-heavy workloads (latency penalty accumulates), data rarely read after write.

section.name: pattern-4-write-behind
section.p: Use write-behind only when eventual consistency is acceptable and write throughput is the primary bottleneck.

rule.statement: Use write-behind only when eventual consistency is acceptable and write throughput is the primary bottleneck. Never use for financial or inventory data without explicit durability guarantees.
rule.rationale: Async DB persistence risks data loss on cache failure; the throughput gain is only worth it when loss is tolerable.

list.item: Write flow: App → Cache → Queue → DB (asynchronous).
Best for: high write throughput, analytics events, activity logs, eventual consistency acceptable.
Avoid for: critical data (ledger, inventory), strong consistency requirements, systems without a reliable queue to buffer writes.
Write flow: App → DB directly. Cache not updated on write. Reads use cache-aside pattern on subsequent access.
Best for: write-once/read-rarely data, large objects (media files, backups), data unlikely to be re-read soon.
Avoid for: data frequently accessed immediately after write — causes cache misses and DB load spikes.

section.name: pattern-selection-matrix
section.p: Quick reference for selecting the right pattern per data type:

list.item: User profiles → Cache-Aside + TTL: read-heavy, tolerate staleness.
Shopping cart → Write-Through: consistency critical, read immediately after write.
Analytics events → Write-Behind: high volume, eventual consistency acceptable.
File uploads → Write-Around: large, rarely re-read soon.
Session data → Read-Through: transparent management, high hit rate.
Leaderboards → Cache-Aside + Event invalidation: frequent reads, periodic updates.
Configuration/feature flags → Cache-Aside + short TTL: rarely changes, safe to serve slightly stale.
Auth lookups → Cache-Aside + short TTL: high frequency, low staleness tolerance.

section.name: cache-key-design
section.p: Proper key construction enables predictable invalidation and long-term maintainability:

rule.statement: Use hierarchical, predictable key patterns. Include version suffix (v1, v2) for keys tied to a schema; never include unstable attributes (timestamps, request IDs, nonces) in keys.
rule.rationale: Predictable keys allow prefix-based invalidation (SCAN + DEL pattern), version suffixes enable cheap schema-change rollover without manual key purge.

list.item: Simple entity: user:{user_id} — maps directly to a single record.
Nested entity: user:{user_id}:orders:page:{page} — hierarchical for pagination.
With version: user:{user_id}:profile:v2 — bump v2 after schema change; old v1 keys expire via TTL.
Complex parameters: search:{md5(sorted_query_params)} — hash to bound key length; sort params first for determinism.
Forbidden in keys: timestamps, request IDs, random nonces, user-agent strings — these produce unique keys on every call, making the cache useless.

rule.statement: Never cache user-specific data with a global key. Always include the user or tenant identifier in the key.
rule.rationale: Global keys for personalized data cause cross-user data leaks; this is a security bug, not just a stale-data bug.

section.name: ttl-guidelines
section.p: TTL strategy directly impacts cache freshness and operational load:

rule.statement: Always add jitter to TTLs (base * (1 + rand(-0.1, 0.1))) to avoid synchronized expiry storms across horizontally scaled instances.
rule.rationale: Without jitter, all instances that populated the cache at the same time expire simultaneously, causing a thundering herd on the DB.

list.item: Static assets (CSS/JS): 1 year — versioned filenames ensure cache busting on deploy.
Product catalog: 5–15 min — moderate change frequency; stale for seconds is acceptable.
User profiles: 1–5 min — personal data, changes occasionally, staleness is visible to the user.
Session data: 30 min–24h — security vs UX balance; sliding expiry on activity.
Search results: 1–5 min — freshness matters, cache to absorb repeated identical queries.
Configuration / feature flags: 1–5 min — short TTL prevents stale config from causing outages.
Real-time data: 10–60 sec — near real-time; cache only if DB/API pressure is demonstrated.
Auth tokens / permissions: match the token TTL — never cache longer than the token is valid.

section.name: hit-ratio-targets
section.p: Monitor hit ratio per key prefix, not in aggregate:

list.item: CDN static assets: >95% — anything below indicates poor cache-control headers or key fragmentation.
API responses: >70% — baseline target; below this the cache rarely pays back its operational cost.
Database query cache: >80% — significant load reduction; below 60% the cache is adding latency without benefit.
Session data: >90% — user-specific, near-deterministic access; low ratio indicates short TTL or key churn.

section.note: Track hit ratio per key prefix, not in aggregate. An overall 80% can hide a dead cache on a hot path masking a burned-out 20% of traffic.

section.name: common-problems-and-solutions
section.p: Six critical failure modes and their solutions:

rule.statement: Cache stampede (thundering herd) must be mitigated with single-flight / request coalescing, not solely with probabilistic early expiration. Both defenses are needed at scale.
rule.rationale: XFetch (probabilistic early expiration) reduces stampede probability but does not eliminate it at high concurrency; single-flight collapses concurrent misses to one upstream fetch.

list.item: Cache Stampede: multiple processes attempt to rebuild the same cache entry simultaneously on expiry. Solutions: single-flight (collapse concurrent misses), probabilistic early expiration (XFetch), distributed lock (SETNX + TTL) during fill.

Hot Keys: single key receives disproportionate read traffic, pinning one Redis shard. Solutions: local in-process L1 cache in front of Redis, key replication to N shards with random suffix selection, read-aside from replicas.

Cold Start: cache empty after deploy or failover; all requests hit DB. Solutions: cache warming script runs before traffic is routed, gradual traffic shift (canary) during startup, pre-populate from a recent dump.

Stale Data: TTL too long or event invalidation missed. Solutions: shorten TTL, implement reliable CDC-based event invalidation (Debezium / outbox pattern), version-based keys for schema-driven invalidation.

Memory Pressure: cache approaching maxmemory limit, triggering evictions that hurt hit ratio. Solutions: set explicit eviction policy (allkeys-lru or volatile-ttl), monitor eviction rate (not just memory %), tier cold data to a cheaper store.

Inconsistency: write-through inconsistency between cache and DB. Solutions: use distributed transactions (rare), accept eventual consistency with bounded TTL, use version keys so stale data auto-expires.

Stale Negatives: caching "not found" causes ghost entries after a subsequent write creates the record. Solution: choose explicitly whether to cache misses; if yes, use very short TTL (5–30 sec) and invalidate on create.
Redis: general purpose, rich data structures (strings, hashes, sorted sets, streams, bitmaps). Single-threaded event loop; use Redis Cluster for horizontal write scaling. Best for most use cases.

Memcached: pure key-value, no persistence, no data structures. Multi-threaded; higher raw throughput for simple get/set. Best for homogeneous string-value caches at very high RPS where Redis memory overhead matters.

Dragonfly: Redis-compatible protocol, multi-threaded (no GIL). Significantly lower memory usage via Dashtable. Best for replacing Redis when single-threaded CPU is the bottleneck. Smaller community; verify compatibility before adoption.

rule.statement: Choose Redis unless profiling demonstrates that single-threaded CPU is the bottleneck and Memcached or Dragonfly is validated by a PoC. Do not migrate for theoretical performance gains.
rule.rationale: Redis ecosystem maturity (client libs, monitoring, Cluster, Sentinel, persistence) reduces operational risk; switching for undemonstrated gains introduces compatibility risk.

section.name: redis-production-rules
section.p: Critical configuration and monitoring practices for production Redis:

list.item: Set maxmemory and an explicit eviction policy: allkeys-lru (general), allkeys-lfu (hot data), volatile-ttl (TTL-keyed eviction). Without a policy, Redis returns OOM errors instead of evicting.
Monitor eviction rate (evicted_keys / sec from INFO stats), not just memory percent. Rising eviction rate means the cache is thrashing.
Disable persistence (save "" and appendonly no) for pure caches — persistence adds latency and is unnecessary when the DB is the source of truth.
Use connection pooling at the application level; each process should hold a pool of 10–50 connections, not one per request.
Use SLOWLOG to surface commands exceeding a latency threshold. The default threshold (10ms) is appropriate for most setups.
Set tcp-keepalive 60 to detect dead connections from application-side network failures.

section.name: cdn-providers
section.p: CDN selection matrix by use case:

list.item: Cloudflare: free tier, Workers for edge logic, DDoS protection, built-in security. Best for most projects — lowest barrier to entry, global coverage.
CloudFront (AWS): deep AWS integration, Lambda@Edge for custom logic, OAI/OAC for S3. Best for AWS-heavy stacks where unified billing and IAM matter.
Fastly: real-time purge API (<150ms), VCL (Varnish Configuration Language) for advanced edge logic. Best when programmatic cache invalidation is a core requirement.
Akamai: enterprise SLAs, broadest PoP coverage, premium pricing. Best for large enterprises with compliance requirements or geographic edge cases.

section.note: For most new projects: Cloudflare first. Migrate to Fastly only when real-time purge latency matters; migrate to CloudFront only when AWS integration is a hard requirement.

list.item: Django: django.core.cache + django-redis backend. Use KEY_PREFIX to namespace keys per environment. cache_page decorator for view-level caching, cache.get_or_set for entity-level.
FastAPI: no built-in cache; use fastapi-cache2 (decorator-based) or aiocache (async). Annotate endpoints with @cache() and set expire parameter.
Express (Node.js): no built-in cache; use ioredis client directly or node-cache for in-process TTL cache. Middleware pattern for route-level caching.
Spring Boot: Spring Cache abstraction (@Cacheable, @CacheEvict, @CachePut) + spring-data-redis. CacheManager bean configures TTL per cache name.

rule.statement: In Django, set KEY_PREFIX to the environment name (prod, staging, dev) in CACHES settings. Without a prefix, a staging deploy can read or pollute production cache keys if they share a Redis instance.
rule.rationale: Shared Redis instances across environments without key namespacing cause data leaks between environments and unpredictable test results.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use a designer agent to map cache layers (CDN → gateway → app → DB) and pick a pattern per data type, a code-author agent to wire the chosen client (Redis/Memcached/Varnish/CDN), and a critic agent to attack invalidation, stampedes, and consistency. Always gather two metrics before/after: cache hit ratio and p95 latency on the cached path.

list.item: Recommended subagents: faion-brainstorm (diverge over cache placement and pattern choice), faion-sdd-execution (produce spec/design including invalidation events and TTL policy), faion-improver (post-deploy audit of hit ratio, eviction rate, p99 tail).

section.p: Prompt pattern for LLM-assisted cache design:
section.code: For each entity in <model>, output a row: | entity | access pattern | volatility | strategy | layer | TTL | invalidation trigger | stampede control | Reject any row where invalidation = "manual" without an event source.

Critique this cache design for: stampedes, hot keys, stale-after-write, negative caching, multi-region replication lag, and TTL synchronization. Suggest concrete code changes (single-flight, jitter, request coalescing).

list.item: Always add jitter to TTLs (`base * (1 + rand(-0.1, 0.1))`) to avoid synchronized expiry storms.
Cache the result of an expensive computation, not the raw row — survives schema changes better.
Set explicit eviction policy (`maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru` or `volatile-ttl`) and alert on eviction rate, not just memory.
Track hit ratio per key prefix; an aggregate hit ratio masks dead caches.
Use idempotent keys; never include unstable attributes (timestamps, request IDs) in keys.
Prefer `SETNX` + TTL for distributed locks during cache fill, not application-level mutexes that don't survive process restarts.
For LLM apps, always measure cache value with a tracing tool — agents tend to over-cache prompt fragments that change every call.
Agents will cache user-specific data with a global key — always require the prompt to spell out the cache key formula (`user:{uid}:profile:v1`).
Agents forget to bump cache version when schema changes; require a `v<N>` suffix in keys and have a critic check schema↔key version coupling.
Don't let agents cache 404s without an explicit policy — silent stale negatives cause "ghost user" bugs.
LLM-generated Redis pipelines often lack `await` boundaries; pin the prompt to specific async patterns of the client lib.
Human-in-loop gates: TTL policy approval (per data class), invalidation strategy review for any write path, post-deploy hit-ratio review.

template.purpose: Generic Python CacheService with get/set/get-or-set-with-lock, XFetch early-expiration, and pattern invalidation.
Production Redis standalone config with maxmemory, eviction policy, TLS, and persistence disabled.
Django CACHES settings for default + sessions with django-redis, KEY_PREFIX, and cache decorators.
